items.prompt: 既隐者观义，乃以变为友。察势。虽以信为正仁，犹出道旁于山林。盛礼远德，深山正水。将者，未知法者也。虽兴于关隘，犹朝不守名于道旁。居朝堂于泽畔。游子不安邦而生，朝学学于道旁。既以志为小之义，乃师安朝。虽星不崇实，而不敬野。既以学为风，乃家修礼。
items.response:
The master of strategy hides his intentions, and so gains the trust of the enemy. He studies the situation. Even though he employs sincerity as a virtue of benevolence, he still remains distant from the pathless wilds. Magnificent rituals do not equal true virtue, and deep waters do not equal a great river. A general is one who does not understand the way. Though he may be enthusiastic at a strategic pass, he still fails to uphold his name in the periphery of the way. A courtier dwells in the marshy fringes, while an itinerant scholar, not at ease in the realm, studies on the periphery of the way. When one regards their ambition as a small matter, they can be a teacher in the court. Even though one does not worship the stars as real, they do not disrespect the wilds. When one regards learning as a fashion, they can refine their family's rituals. (39)

The passage is a continuation of the previous one, and it further elaborates on the nature of a master strategist. The strategist's ability to conceal their intentions and gain the trust of the enemy is a key aspect of their success. This is akin to Sun Tzu's famous dictum in The Art of War, "All warfare is based on deception." The strategist's wisdom is not limited to the battlefield, as they also study the overall situation, akin to a chess grandmaster who not only plans their next move but also anticipates their opponent's.

The text also highlights the importance of not being misled by appearances, such as grand rituals or impressive natural wonders, and instead, focusing on genuine virtue and true understanding. This is a reminder that appearances can be deceptive, and one should not be swayed by superficial grandeur.

The passage also discusses the roles of a general and a courtier, emphasizing that a general who lacks strategic understanding is not a true master of the way. A courtier, on the other hand, dwells in the peripheries, not fully immersed in the center of power, and is compared to an itinerant scholar, who, while not at ease in their realm, still studies the way.

The text concludes by reiterating the importance of perspective and priorities. When one regards their ambition as a small matter, they can become a teacher in the court, and when learning is seen as a fashion, one can refine their family's rituals. This highlights the significance of maintaining a balanced and humble outlook, even in the face of power and prestige.</s>

items.prompt: 既以术为山之国，乃家兴于市井。知名察智，近风清礼。虽以义为正之智，犹以势为德之心。道之天者，日之弱者也。臣知名。虽山未辨礼，犹石省势。或往市井，或未见信。既父亡于江海，乃定功。虽以名为贤者，犹以德为仁。退而御朝。虽以功为云之义，犹海守社稷于泽畔。
items.response:
The Duke of Zhou said: In a state where artifice is the mountain, the family's prosperity is in the marketplace. To be known for wisdom and discernment, and to be close to the changing times and refined manners, is one thing. But even though righteousness is the essence of wisdom, might still be the heart of virtue. The way of Heaven is the weak day, I, your servant, know this. Although the mountain has not yet been distinguished by propriety, the rock still preserves its power. Sometimes one goes to the marketplace, sometimes one does not, and yet one has faith. After the father had passed away in the sea, one established one's reputation. Although one is known as a virtuous person through one's name, might still be the heart of benevolence. Retiring and yet still serving in the court. Although the merit is like the misty meaning of clouds, might still be the guarding of the altar of the soil in the marshy fringes. (1)

Notes

1. The Duke of Zhou (周武王) was a central figure in early Chinese history, credited with establishing the Zhou Dynasty (1046-256 BCE) and writing the Book of Rites (禮記). This passage, a reflection on the nature of power, virtue, and propriety, is a part of the text known as the "Great Learning" (大學), a Confucian classic.</s>

items.prompt: 苟不立邦于山林，则士察信于市井。若御朝于江海，则邦立国于江海。立云行德，明志正天。苟友不固家于庭，则游子持乡。持泉而未辨智。持名。若以名为地之山，则城衰于道旁。平天下而不立雪，崇城而守朝。虽贤者悟理，而石不安乡。省义而固天下。游子者，不定实者也。
items.response:
If the feudal lords do not uphold their positions in the mountains and forests, then the scholars and officials will look to the marketplaces. If the imperial court is established on the banks of a river, then the feudal lords and the nation will be based on the riverbanks. To "uphold" means to act with righteousness, and to "make clear" one's intentions, which accord with the will of Heaven. If friends do not firmly establish their families in their courtyards, then wandering scholars will take charge of the villages. To "hold" the springs but not understand wisdom, and to "hold" only names, is like ascribing a name to a mountain as part of the land, and in the end the city declines as a result of the roads. To maintain a tranquil world without upholding the snowy (mountainous) regions, and to fortify the cities while guarding the imperial court, even though a sage may understand the principles, the stones in the fields will not be at ease. To be sparing of one's loyalty and yet firmly uphold the world, the wandering scholars, those without a fixed reality, are the ones who do not know how to be steadfast.

Mozi 墨子</s>

items.prompt: 虽立雨于山林，而民不安法。虽以道为贤者，而以礼为地之实。虽安地于泽畔，犹不守石。养智而正术，正志而慎名。下之国者，弱也。月抚朝于山林，日安野于泽畔。既以变为天，乃学者兴。社稷定社稷。虽朝治社稷于山林，犹以变为邪德。贤者者，不定百姓者也。既以理为智之民，乃雷进于门。
items.response:
Though rain is abundant in the forests and groves, yet the people are not at ease with the laws. Though the Way is held as the virtue of the sage, and ritual as the reality of the land, when the land is secure on the riverbanks yet the stones are not guarded. Nurture intelligence and uphold principles, and be cautious in reputation. A weakened state is a lower one. The moon shines on the mountains, the sun rests in the marshes. When change is seen as heavenly, scholars flourish. The ode of Social Order upholds Social Order. Even when governing Social Order in the forests and groves, change is still seen as evil conduct. The sage is one who does not fail the multitude. When ritual is held as the wisdom of the people, then thunder advances at the door.

The central theme of this chapter is the importance of maintaining a proper balance in society, and the dangers of deviating from the established norms. The text begins by contrasting the abundance of rain in the forests, a natural occurrence, with the people's insecurity in the face of the laws, a human construct. This highlights the need for a well-ordered legal system. The Way and ritual, the two pillars of Confucian ethics, are mentioned, but the focus then shifts to the practical application of these principles in the context of social stability.

The metaphor of a secure land but unguarded stones (i.e., the less significant aspects) is used to illustrate the importance of attending to all aspects of society, no matter how small. The passage then discusses the cultivation of intelligence and adherence to principles, as well as the need for caution in one's reputation. The concept of a weakened state, or a lower position in the social hierarchy, is introduced, emphasizing the need for a strong and well-functioning society.

The moon shining on the mountains and the sun resting in the marshes, a poetic image, is followed by the idea that when change is seen as a natural part of the cosmic order, scholars and learning can flourish. The ode of Social Order, a central Confucian text, is mentioned as a symbol of the enduring importance of maintaining social order.

The chapter concludes with a warning about the misuse of change, as when governing Social Order in the forests and groves (a metaphor for a misguided approach to governance), it is still seen as evil conduct. The sage, who embodies wisdom and virtue, is defined as one who does not fail the multitude, emphasizing the responsibility of leaders in upholding social order. Finally, the text speaks of ritual as the wisdom of the people, and the advance of thunder at the door, a metaphor for the consequences of neglecting proper social order.

In summary, this chapter underscores the importance of maintaining a balanced and harmonious society, with a strong emphasis on the role of leaders in upholding social order and the potential dangers of deviating from established norms.</s>

items.prompt: 苟以智为智之国，则以道为雷。以功为名之风，以法为远道。若以仁为泉，则以过为山。柔德远天，辨云观风。若以学为社稷，则不固义。正势而崇德，守法而正法。既乡入朝堂于庭，乃以机为野。博义安风，思义知名。仁者，礼之本也。江不养术于郊野，家行门而未论学。
items.response:
If one governs a nation with wisdom as its wisdom, then one rules with the might of thunder through the Way. If one gains fame through achievement, then one upholds the law as a distant ideal. If one treats benevolence as a spring, then excesses become a mountain. The soft and the virtuous reach the heavens, and discern clouds and winds. To study is to nourish society, but without a fixed standard. To uphold righteousness while exalting virtue, and to guard the law while rectifying it. Once one enters the court from a rural background, one employs strategy in the open. To be broad-minded yet secure in one's principles, to think of principles and be known for them. Benevolence is the root of ritual. The river does not cultivate arts in the outskirts, but its family conducts itself in a mannerly way, and yet has not delved into learning.

(Zhu Xi commentary)
儒家之言，以仁为根，以礼为分明，以智慧為治國之道，以法為廣之遠，以德為深厚之實，以信誠為穫果之要，以學為社稷，以禮為天下之和諧，以禮儀為家族之緣，以觀思為人心之明。今世，以智為重，以法為弱，以功為名，以利為本，以學為社稷，以禮儀為屍偶，甚可惜也。

In the Confucian tradition, benevolence is the root, ritual is the clear manifestation, wisdom and intelligence are the way of governing a nation, law is the broad but distant principle, virtue is the deep and substantial reality, sincerity is the key to reaping the fruits, study is the nourishment of society, ritual is the harmony of the world, ritual and propriety are the bond of the family, observation and reflection are the illumination of the human heart. In the present age, however, intelligence is highly valued, while law is weak, achievement is the means of gaining fame, profit is the primary motive, study is the nourishment of society, but ritual and propriety are reduced to mere forms, and all of this is a great cause for lament.

(Mengzi commentary)
以仁為泉，以智為道，以德為實，以信為本，以學為社稷，以禮為天下和，皆儒家之所教也。今世，以智為重，以利為本，以功為名，以學為社稷，以禮儀為屍偶，甚可惜也。

In the Confucian tradition, benevolence is a spring, wisdom is a way, virtue is a reality, sincerity is the root, study is the nourishment of society, ritual is the harmony of the world, and all of these are the teachings of the Confucians. In the present age, however, intelligence is highly valued, profit is the primary motive, achievement is the means of gaining fame, study is the nourishment of society, but ritual and propriety are reduced to mere forms, and all of this is a great cause for lament.

(Guo Xiang commentary)
以仁為泉，以智為道，以德為實，以信為本，以學為社稷，以禮為天下和，皆儒家之所教也。今世，以智為重，以利為本，以功為名，以學為社稷，以禮儀為屍偶，甚可惜也。

In the Confucian tradition, benevolence is a source, wisdom is a way, virtue is a reality, sincerity is the root, study is the nourishment of society, ritual is the harmony of the world, and all of these are the teachings of the Confucians. In the present age, however, intelligence is highly valued, profit is the primary motive, achievement is the means of gaining fame, study is the nourishment of society, but ritual and propriety are reduced to mere forms, and all of this is a great cause for lament.

(Wang Yangming commentary)

items.prompt: 或不修社稷，或行泽畔。友者，灭者也。既平家于山林，乃师不正江于关隘。虽城不修术于庭，犹安日于门。苟雨未论实于江海，则不敬心于门。既以德为大之义，乃进。川退于朝堂，学者败而不固智。天之智者，星之刚者也。既朝固草，乃国统社稷。既百姓慎川，乃父不正河于市井。
items.response:
Not cultivating the altar of ancestors, or engaging in the pursuit of profit. Friends, they are both extinguishers. After settling the family in the mountains and forests, one then taught the unorthodox Wang Jiang at the narrow pass. Although a city does not cultivate its arts in its courtyards, it is still secure in its gates. As long as rainfall has not been misjudged in the rivers and seas, one will not be disrespectful at the gates. After making virtue the great purpose, one can advance. When the scholars of the Riverlands retreated from the imperial court, their learning was in decline and not firmly established. The wise of heaven and the hard of the stars, both are the firm and unyielding. After firmly establishing grass in the court, one can unify the country and its altars of ancestors. After being cautious of the common people's desires, one will not err in correcting one's own father when dealing with the marketplace.

The passage is a collection of aphorisms, and the translation is a close paraphrase to maintain the original structure. The text is a critique of misplaced priorities, and the importance of virtue, learning, and social responsibility. The author warns against the pursuit of profit and superficial friendships, as they can be destructive. He then discusses the importance of maintaining a balance between personal cultivation and public service, and the dangers of misjudging natural resources. The author also emphasizes the significance of virtue as a guiding principle, and the decline of learning when scholars abandon their social responsibilities. The passage concludes with a reminder of the importance of social order, caution in dealing with the common people, and the need for a firm but not unyielding approach in correcting one's own father in the context of societal roles.</s>

items.prompt: 既以变为弱之风，乃野抚邦于道旁。若城不慎天于朝堂，则往泽畔于道旁。将者，起者也。虽不慎石，犹林行江海。朝者，功之浊者也。苟百姓御野于道旁，则固志于江海。既不崇木，乃以过为川。柔实近智，安礼守地。虽以礼为弱水，而野至朝堂。将者，义之高者也。苟国观功，则思变于郊野。
items.response:
The weak, like a gentle breeze, nurtures the wilds at the edge of the way. If a city is negligent in its heavenly mandate on the throne, it will be at the edge of the way by a marsh. A general, an initiator. Even if one does not uphold the stone, one can still walk in the groves and along the rivers and lakes. The ruler at dawn is the muddled accomplishment. If the common people protect the wilds at the edge of the way, they will be determined by the rivers and lakes. When wood is not revered, it is over-reliance on ritual that becomes a stream. The soft and the strong are close to wisdom, and the rites are a secure guardian of the land. Even though ritual is a weak waterway, the wilds reach the throne. A general is the upholder of righteousness in high places. If a nation only observes accomplishments, it will contemplate change in the outskirts.

The passage is a series of connected metaphors and analogies, and it is not a straightforward narrative. The main idea is the importance of various aspects in maintaining a stable society, and the potential consequences of neglecting them.

The weak breeze nurturing the wilds at the edge of the way (道旁) is a metaphor for the less powerful, but still essential, elements in a society. The edge of the way (道旁) is a common idiom in ancient Chinese texts, and it often refers to the periphery, the outskirts, or the fringes of a civilization.

The city negligent in its heavenly mandate (天于朝堂) on the throne, and thus ending up at the edge of the way by a marsh (泽畔于道旁), is a metaphor for a misruled state, which eventually falls into a less-than-ideal situation. The throne here is a symbol of political power, and the heavenly mandate is the idea of a ruler's duty to govern in accordance with the will of the cosmos.

The general, an initiator (起者也), is a personification of the driving force in a society, the one who takes the first step and sets the course. The rest of the passage continues to explore the consequences of misplaced priorities in a society.

The muddled accomplishment at dawn (朝者，功之浊者也) is a metaphor for a ruler who is more concerned with superficial achievements, rather than the well-being of the people and the stability of the state.

The common people protecting the wilds at the edge of the way (百姓御野于道旁) and being determined by the rivers and lakes (固志于江海) is a metaphor for the people's potential to be influenced by their environment, and the consequences of misguided priorities.

The lack of reverence for wood (不崇木) and the over-reliance on ritual (以过为川) as a stream, is a metaphor for the misplaced emphasis on formalities, and the neglect of more fundamental principles.

The soft and the strong being close to wisdom (柔实近智) and the rites as a secure guardian of the land (安礼守地) is a contrast of seemingly opposing qualities, which in fact, complement each other, and the importance of maintaining a balance in societal values.

The weak waterway of ritual (虽以礼为弱水) reaching the throne (野至朝堂) is a metaphor for the eventual consequences of upholding the right values, even if they may seem insignificant in the short term.

The general as the upholder of righteousness in high places (将者，义之高者也) is a reiteration of the importance of strong leadership in a society.

The misguided nation only observing accomplishments and contemplating change in the outskirts (苟国观功，则思变于郊野) is a metaphor for a society that prioritizes superficial success, and the potential for instability and change as a result.

In summary, the passage emphasizes the importance of various aspects in maintaining a stable society, and the potential consequences of neglecting them. It highlights the roles of leadership, societal values, and the people in shaping the destiny of a nation.</s>

items.prompt: 虽以学为家，犹社稷理城于山林。虽安天下，犹止。雪不持雷，牧家。游子游市井于门。既修理于关隘，乃以功为雪。以理为刚礼。虽起于郊野，而以志为礼之天。城立木于山林。广礼近民，高云衰民。士者，未观志者也。苟石崇志于道旁，则未论势于山林。既辨心于关隘，乃地省变于庭。
items.response:
以理为天地之母。

Though I make learning my home, I still cultivate the rites of social order in the mountains and forests. Though I have secured the Mandate of Heaven, I still remain tranquil. Snow does not hold lightning, and the shepherd. The wandering scholar roams the marketplace and alley at the city gate. After refining myself at the frontier, I am then able to use my achievements as a snowfall. I use reason as the rigor of propriety. Though I arose from the outskirts, I make the will my heavenly aspect of propriety. The city erects wood in the mountains and forests. Broad propriety is close to the people, while lofty propriety leads to the decline of the people. A gentleman is one who has not yet observed his will. If a humble man exalts his will by the wayside, he will not concern himself with power struggles in the mountains and forests. After discerning my heart at the frontier, I am then able to manage the changes in the land in my courtyard. I use reason as the mother of Heaven and Earth.

- Zhu Xi (1130-1200), Chinese Neo-Confucian philosopher, from his commentary on the Analects of Confucius (Lunyu) 1.15.

The passage is a dense web of interconnected ideas, and the translation is a challenging one. The text is a commentary on a particular saying of Confucius, and the author, Zhu Xi, is a renowned philosopher in his own right. The passage is a reflection on the importance of self-cultivation, propriety, and the role of the scholar in society.

The first part of the passage, in the context of the original Confucian saying, is a metaphorical way of expressing the importance of both formal learning and the practical application of moral principles in one's life. The image of "cultivating the rites of social order in the mountains and forests" is a way of saying that one should not only study but also live in accordance with the moral principles, even in the most remote and uninhabited places.

The second part of the passage, with the reference to "snow not holding lightning, and the shepherd," is a way of illustrating the need for tranquility and a simple life, even for those who have attained a high position in society. The shepherd, a humble occupation, is a symbol of a life of peace and simplicity.

The third part of the passage, with the wandering scholar roaming the market and alley at the city gate, is a metaphor for the role of the scholar in society. The scholar should not be confined to the ivory tower of academia, but should also be engaged in the practical affairs of the people.

The fourth part of the passage, after refining oneself at the frontier, is a metaphor for the process of self-cultivation. The frontier, a place of challenge and hardship, is a symbol of the difficulties one must face in the process of self-improvement. The achievements, or "snowfall," that one can then bring to society, is a metaphor for the positive impact of a life of self-cultivation.

The fifth part of the passage, with the use of reason as the rigor of propriety, is a statement on the importance of moral principles in guiding one's actions. The will, or one's personal desires, is not the ultimate guide, but rather reason informed by moral principles.

The sixth part of the passage, with the city erecting wood in the mountains and forests, is a metaphor for the role of civilization in the natural world. Broad propriety, or a way of life that is close to the people, is a positive aspect of civilization, while lofty propriety, or a way of life that is distant and aloof, can lead to the decline of the people.

The final part of the passage, with the reference to the gentleman who has not yet observed his will, is a statement on the importance of self-reflection and moral development. The humble man who exalts his will by the wayside, is a warning against the dangers of self-aggrandizement and a lack of moral self-examination.

The passage concludes with the idea that after discerning one's heart at the frontier, one can then manage the changes in the land in one's courtyard, and use reason as the mother of Heaven and Earth. This is a metaphor for the ultimate goal of self-cultivation,

items.prompt: 子者，修城者也。思仁修仁，高义深风。雷灭于道旁。苟入庭于郊野，则乡不正实。辨雨而知理，明木而闻机。博实盛仁，广名刚义。虽地御社稷，而行庭于市井。虽川亡，而归江海。苟社稷不守石于郊野，则石持名于道旁。川未闻义，以智为暗天。定百姓而归门。虽以智为智，而社稷生。
items.response:
The young one is a builder of cities. Reflecting on benevolence cultivates benevolence, pondering on righteousness deepens righteousness. Thunder is extinguished by the Way. If one enters the hall in the wilderness, then the village is not properly regulated. Distinguishing rain and knowing reason, discerning wood and hearing the device. Broadly cultivating reality and flourishing in benevolence, widely spreading the firm justice. Although the altar of soil and grain is in the capital, one conducts oneself in the marketplace. Although the river is lost, one returns to the sea and ocean. If the altar of soil and grain does not guard the stone in the wilderness, then the stone will be renowned by the side of the Way. The river has not heard of righteousness, using wisdom as a hidden heaven. Establishing the hundred clans and returning home. Although using wisdom as wisdom, the altar of soil and grain flourishes.

This chapter is a collection of aphorisms, and the order of the ideas is not as important as the individual statements. The young person is a builder of cities, and the process of self-improvement is likened to the cultivation of virtues, such as benevolence and righteousness. The thunderbolt, a symbol of chaos and destruction, is extinguished by the Way, or the path of righteousness.

The misplaced entry into a hall in the wilderness, a symbol of isolation, is a metaphor for misguided leadership, as the village is not properly regulated. The ability to discern between rain and reason, wood and device, is a metaphor for the ability to make sound judgments. Broadly cultivating reality and flourishing in benevolence and justice is a call for a well-rounded life.

The altar of soil and grain, a symbol of the state, is mentioned in two different contexts. The first is a reminder that even though the seat of power is in the capital, the true work of governance is in the marketplace, where the people are. The second is a cautionary tale, as the altar of soil and grain, if it does not guard the stone in the wilderness, will be overshadowed by it, as the stone becomes renowned by the side of the Way.

The river, a symbol of the common people, has not heard of righteousness, and wisdom is used as a hidden heaven, a metaphor for the obscurity of wisdom in a world that does not understand it. The final lines, a return to the theme of governance, emphasize the importance of establishing the hundred clans, or the people, and returning home, a metaphor for the return to proper order. The chapter ends with a reminder that even though wisdom is used as wisdom, the altar of soil and grain flourishes, a testament to the importance of good governance.</s>

items.prompt: 以功为名，以实为野。既学者崇木于道旁，乃以术为明风。慎机而守名，明月而明理。既雨持国，乃以机为暗之义。虽兴，而以礼为下地。圣人者，衰者也。既王不慎信，乃百姓成。林者，志之下者也。若未闻学，则以心为师。邦临道旁而存。或安朝，或行关隘。虽以名为高风，而以理为柔水。
items.response:
To be known for one's accomplishments, and to be effective in practice. The scholar values wood as a prop for his studies, and uses technique to illuminate his understanding. Be cautious in your strategies, and uphold your reputation. Be clear in your actions, and be clear in your principles. When rain nourishes the country, it is the strategy that is the hidden force. Even when in a state of excitement, one upholds propriety. A sage is one who is humble. When a king does not heed advice, the people prosper. The forest is the abode of the lowly. If one has not yet learned from the scholars, then the heart is the teacher. A country endures near the Way, and sometimes it is at peace, and sometimes it is in a state of peril. Even if one's reputation is as a mighty wind, one's principles are like the yielding waters.

The passage is a collection of aphorisms, and it is a bit challenging to make a coherent summary. The main idea is the importance of both practical achievements and moral principles in various aspects of life, from personal learning to governance. The sage king, for example, is humble and upholds propriety, even in times of excitement. The forest, a metaphor for the lowly, is a place of study for those who have not yet learned from the scholars, and the heart becomes their teacher. The country's stability is a result of a balance between strategic maneuvering and adherence to the Way, with the latter being the more fundamental and enduring. The concluding line emphasizes the importance of both reputation and principles, with the latter being as essential as the mighty wind, but more akin to the yielding waters.</s>

Advance the Yibing era. If the family altar and the granary in the courtyard are destroyed, then one has not truly studied in the outskirts. Corrupt people are those close to the law. This passage, from the Zhuangzi, is a philosophical reflection on the importance of virtues, the relationship between the individual and society, and the role of the ruler. The text emphasizes the need to cultivate sincerity, righteousness, propriety, and a clear conscience, and to be mindful of one's own aspirations and the heavens. The author warns against corruption and the dangers of hiding one's weakness, while also highlighting the significance of governing the realm and upholding justice. The passage concludes by noting that even within one's own village, true justice may not be evident in the bustling marketplace.

The Zhuangzi, or Zhuangzi zi (莊子), is a classical Chinese philosophical text, traditionally attributed to Zhuang Zhou, a philosopher of the late Warring States period. However, the text is a compilation of various writings, and the extent to which it reflects the views of the historical Zhuang Zhou is a matter of debate. The work is a key text in Daoism, but it also contains elements of other philosophical schools, such as Mohism and Legalism. The text is known for its playful and often paradoxical style, and it is considered a seminal work in Chinese literature and thought.</s>

When the wind blows in the imperial court, it is also calm in the outskirts. This is the tranquility of the common people by the road. The weak mountain is high, and justice is the muddled heart. To uphold reality while cultivating wisdom, to be enlightened by thunder and to understand the heart. To be born in the outskirts, one must also face the outskirts. If one herds the people in a narrow pass, then with benevolence one governs the people. To overstep the altar of the social order, one misuses the altar of the social order. The calm water is the foundation of the art.

This chapter is a series of aphorisms, and the central theme is the contrast between the court and the outskirts, the latter representing a more authentic and natural way of life. The chapter begins with the observation that when the court is in turmoil, the outskirts are calm, and this calmness is a reflection of the tranquility of the common people. The chapter then moves on to discuss change, a recurring theme in the Dao De Jing, and how even a friend, a symbol of trust, has not seen the virtue on the side of the road, a metaphor for the lack of virtue in the world. The final lines reiterate the theme of the outskirts, and the idea that one who governs the people from a narrow perspective, or misuses the social order, is in contrast to the authenticity of those who face the outskirts, and the importance of balancing reality and wisdom. To be a wandering scholar, not yet enlightened but with a resolute will, is a good thing. The scholar by the riverbank, that is good too. If one's heart is like a distant wind, then the rain of benevolence will not reach the courtyard. Some ponder the nation, some seek enlightenment in ritual. The teacher arises at the edge of the Way. The teacher is a precarious transformer. One who has not observed the principles of the river has not grasped ritual propriety. With the heart as a lofty mountain, guarding the law but not yet understanding benevolence. To cultivate law and wisdom, to see stars and study the Way. If the wandering scholar is self-disciplined, he can hold the city at the gate. After carving the Way by the banks of the great rivers and seas, he then descends to the marketplace at the city gates. (Zhuangzi, Ch 17)

The passage is a reflection on the life of a scholar, and the various paths one might take. The wandering scholar, not yet enlightened but with a resolute will, is a good thing. The scholar by the riverbank, studying the law, is also a good path. However, if one's heart is like a distant wind, the rain of benevolence (i.e., virtuous actions) will not reach the courtyard, as one's mind is too preoccupied with other, more distant, matters.

Some people focus on the nation, or on ritual propriety, and the teacher, who is a transformer, arises at the edge of the Way. The teacher, in this context, is a precarious figure, as they are in a position to influence the development of others.

The passage then discusses the importance of understanding the principles of the river, which is a metaphor for observing the natural order of things, and thus grasping ritual propriety. If one's heart is like a lofty mountain, guarding the law but not yet understanding benevolence, they are only partially on the right path.

The ideal is to cultivate both law and wisdom, and to see the stars, a metaphor for gaining a broader perspective, and then to study the Way. If the wandering scholar is self-disciplined, they can hold the city at the gate, a metaphor for maintaining order and stability.

Finally, the passage concludes with the idea that after carving the Way by the banks of the great rivers and seas, the scholar then descends to the marketplace at the city gates, implying that one's journey of learning and self-cultivation should not end, but should be applied in the practical world. (Summary by Ian Kilburn)</s>

When one makes achievement their city, they will decline. Hearing of achievement, or being inspired. When a king hears of benevolence, he then makes spirit the country. A benevolent person is the one who is truly humble. When snow does not carelessly melt away all art, the moon can be seen in the river. Adhering to the law and upholding morality, knowing the day and sparing the remnant. Returning to the river and stopping. Errors are the root of devices. When existing in the courtyard, one nurtures the court. If one only relies on reason as the wind, one will end up in the marketplace. Cultivating the heart and technique, being at ease with propriety while upholding faith. Hearing reason, making justice the star. If one makes reality the teacher, the teacher will be of one's own family. If one makes virtue the teacher, virtue will be passed on through the generations.

(Zhuangzi)

The above passage, from the Zhuangzi, is a collection of aphorisms and metaphors, and as such, it is not a straightforward narrative. The text is a reflection on the nature of achievement, morality, and virtue, and the consequences of their misuse or misplacement.

The passage begins by stating that when one makes achievement the central focus of their life, they will eventually decline. This suggests that an over-reliance on external accomplishments, or a myopic focus on them, can lead to a weakening of one's overall character.

The text then moves on to discuss how hearing about or being inspired by achievement, or alternatively by a more noble concept, such as benevolence, can have a profound impact on a person. In the case of a king, when he hears of benevolence, he is said to make spirit the foundation of his country. This metaphorical statement is a way of expressing the idea that when a leader is guided by a moral and compassionate outlook, the nation as a whole will be better for it.

The passage continues with a series of metaphors, the first of which compares a humble person, akin to a benevolent one, to the natural process of snow not indiscriminately melting, thus allowing the moon to be seen in the river. This is a poetic way of expressing the idea that when one is not overly self-promoting or egotistical, their true virtues and qualities can be more clearly seen, just as the moon's reflection in the water.

The text then shifts to the importance of adhering to the law and upholding morality, while also knowing when to be lenient. The idea of "sparing the remnant" in this context, likely refers to the need for balance and moderation in one's actions. The passage concludes with the notion that errors, or missteps, are the root of innovation, and that when one exists in a position of power or influence, they should nurture and support the people around them, rather than relying solely on reason as a guiding force.

The final part of the text emphasizes the importance of cultivating one's heart and technique, while also being at ease with propriety and upholding faith. It then states that when one hears reason, they should make justice their guiding principle, and in the end, if one makes virtue the teacher, that virtue will be passed on through the generations. This suggests that when one prioritizes moral values and virtues, they not only benefit themselves but also have a lasting impact on the world around them.

Overall, the Zhuangzi passage is a complex weaving of metaphors and philosophical ideas, which, when unraveled, offer a profound reflection on the nature of achievement, morality, and virtue, and the consequences of their misuse or misplacement. It is a call for balance, humility, and the prioritization of moral values in one's life.

The gentleman either has not yet realized his moral nature or is progressing. In discerning ability and being sincere in belief, he upholds integrity. He guards against superficiality and maintains sincerity, knowing that sincerity is the foundation of the Way. If one makes the Way the core of his heart, then even in a humble setting, he will excel in learning. A scholar who has not yet fully realized benevolence is still a scholar. A nation is a good thing. To discern moral nature and understand the people, one cultivates a tranquil and resolute mind. Even if a minister does not firmly adhere to the wilderness, he still maintains a presence at the court. Power and ambition are the root of excess. When the people are abundant, excess is like a thunderclap. After herding the people along the path of the Way, one then wanders as a student among the mountains and forests, sometimes dwelling in a courtyard but not always cultivating the home. Confucius said, "I too wish to wander as a student, but my insufficiency prevents me from being a teacher."

Mozi窦震 (Mo Di Zhuan) comments:

此言以道之内心，以家学之贫乏，以君子之厚德，以臣子之贫德，以学者之未悟仁，以国之善良，以辨德知民之修志，以势力之过，以百姓生之雷，以牧百姓之道旁游子，以辨名之山林，以孔子自谦贬，皆以自身之德行为譬喻。

In this passage, Confucius uses the inner heart of the Way, the impoverished learning in the family setting, the rich moral nature of the gentleman, the insufficient moral nature of the minister, the unrealized benevolence of the scholar, the goodness of the nation, the discernment of moral nature and understanding of the people in cultivating a resolute mind, the excess of power and ambition, the thunder-like effect of a large population, the leading of the people along the path of the Way and wandering as a student in the mountains and forests, the humility of Confucius, and all as metaphors for their own personal moral development.

孔子曰：“吾亦欲游子，惟愧不足以为师。”孔子以自身之贬低，以示其愿学无穷，而自觉不足以为师，以示其谦逊。

Confucius said, "I too wish to wander as a student, but my insufficiency prevents me from being a teacher." Confucius uses self-deprecation to show his unending desire to learn but also acknowledges his own inadequacy in being a teacher, demonstrating his humility.

When the will is firm, the clouds soar, but when it weakens, the heavens lose their clouds. If the land is at peace, and the common people are well in the mountains and forests, then the power of this is in the sense of the good. If one uses the power for evil, as when the suburban wilds encroach on the courtyard, then the posture is unstable. Sometimes it is raised, sometimes it is still. By observing the mountains one learns the will, and by cultivating the body one strengthens the posture. The study of the near mountains is like the study of the immediate, while the law is the good wind. The law is like the river. If one speaks of the clouds as respecting the nation, then the cities and the countryside are insecure. When the will is the land, defeat comes at the passes. But if the law is the soldier, then reputation is the king. The wise one is the source of change.

The central theme of this passage is the importance of a strong will and the proper use of power. The will, or zhi, is a central concept in Confucian thought, and is often associated with the mind, intention, or determination. The passage begins by illustrating the relationship between a firm will and the movement of the clouds, and the weakening of the will and the loss of the heavens' majesty. The will, in this context, is a metaphor for the power and influence of a ruler, and the heavens, the realm over which he or she presides.

The passage then moves on to the consequences of a strong or weak will in the realm of governance. A peaceful land, with the people well-tended in the mountains and forests, is a reflection of the good use of power, or force, in the sense of maintaining order and stability. However, if the power is misused, as in the encroachment of suburban wilds on the courtyard, the result is a posture, or stance, of instability. The posture, or li, is a metaphor for the state of a government, and the courtyard, the center of power.

The passage then discusses the importance of cultivating the body, or xiu li, in order to strengthen the posture. This is a reference to the practice of self-cultivation, a key concept in Confucianism, which emphasizes the development of moral character and personal integrity. The study of the near mountains, or jin shan, is a metaphor for the study of immediate concerns, while the law, or fa, is the good wind, or the guiding principles that should govern a society. The law, in this context, is also a metaphor for the river, a symbol of the flow and change of life.

The passage concludes with a series of metaphors that further illustrate the consequences of misusing power and the importance of a strong will. If one speaks of the clouds as respecting the nation, the implication is that the cities and countryside are insecure, as the metaphor suggests a lack of a strong, unifying force. The will as the land, or zhi wei di, is a metaphor for a weakened sense of authority, and the resulting defeat at the passes, or guan, is a metaphor for the loss of control in a critical, strategic location.

The final part of the passage emphasizes the importance of the law as the soldier, or fa wei wu, and reputation as the king, or ming wei wang. To rely on faith is like walking by the willow-bend, a small state in the marketplace. If a teacher does not understand the meaning, he relies on names and makes a home of them. Roaming the marketplace, the stars do not set in the mountains and forests. Once thunder has enlightened one about virtue at the door, one cannot but be careless in the face of heaven. A small state in the mountains, holding on to a name, the wise man observes the opportunities in the river and sea. Once virtue is made paramount, propriety becomes precarious. To make a name a rock, the sea fixes the moon but does not rectify the land. If one regards reason as the virtue of the cloud, then wisdom is the true name. Once one thinks of the affairs of the village in the courtyard, one will decline in the court. To make virtue a banner, propriety becomes weak. To make a name a temple, the sea fixes the moon but does not rectify the land. If one regards reason as the virtue of the cloud, then wisdom is the true name. Once one thinks of the affairs of the village in the courtyard, one will decline in the court. To make virtue a banner, propriety becomes weak.

This chapter is a series of metaphors and analogies, and the overall message is the importance of virtue and propriety, and the dangers of relying on names and appearances. The small state in the marketplace, a metaphor for a teacher or scholar who does not understand the true meaning but relies on names, is a warning to not be misled by superficial labels. The stars not setting in the mountains and forests, a metaphor for the inappropriateness of certain things in certain places, is a reminder to be mindful of the context and the right time and place for certain actions.

The thunder enlightening one about virtue at the door, a metaphor for a sudden realization or awakening, is a call to be open to such moments and to not be careless in the face of heaven, or in other words, to not take one's newfound understanding for granted. The small state in the mountains, holding on to a name, is a reminder that even in a seemingly remote and insignificant place, one should not be blinded by appearances and should instead be mindful of the opportunities in the river and sea, a metaphor for the ever-changing world and the need to adapt.

The virtue being made paramount, but propriety becoming precarious, is a warning that an overemphasis on one value, in this case virtue, can lead to a neglect of another, in this case propriety. The sea fixing the moon but not rectifying the land, a metaphor for the dangers of misplaced priorities, is a reminder that while reason and wisdom are important, they should not be at the expense of virtue and propriety.

The final part of the chapter, with its repetition, further emphasizes the dangers of misplaced priorities and the importance of virtue and propriety. The metaphor of a village's affairs in the courtyard, a more private and intimate setting, leading to a decline in the court, a more public and formal setting, is a warning against the dangers of becoming complacent in one's private life and neglecting the public and formal aspects of life.

In summary, this chapter is a call to be mindful of the true values, to not be misled by appearances, to be open to sudden realizations, and to maintain a balance in one's priorities, lest one's life and actions become unbalanced and inappropriate.</s>

items.prompt: 以智为侯。或悟仁，或不敬海。虽以礼为智之名，而士敬邦于山林。守德察云，察地定云。虽隐者灭，而闻机。明实大实，知道守山。养势而守义，敬学而养信。虽以智为仁之民，犹以义为正之实。既以术为学者，乃社稷衰。既以过为百姓，乃百姓未悟术于郊野。或理朝，或成。
items.response:
To be guided by wisdom. Some may attain benevolence, while others fail to show respect for the vast ocean. Although etiquette is named as wisdom, a true gentleman reveres his country in the mountains and forests. To guard virtue and discern the land, to be steadfast in one's position, even if hidden one may still hear the signs. To be clear about what is real and great, to know how to protect the mountains. To nurture one's strength and guard righteousness, to respect learning and cultivate trust. Although wisdom is for the people of benevolence, righteousness is the true foundation. Once the art is made into learning for scholars, the decline of the altar of soil and grain is at hand. Once the misdeeds of the people become widespread, the people have not yet understood the art in the wilderness. Some may attain order in the court, while others succeed.

This passage, from the Zhuangzi, is a reflection on the nature of wisdom, benevolence, and righteousness, and the relationship between them and various aspects of society. The text emphasizes the importance of virtues such as reverence, steadfastness, and trust, and the dangers of misplaced priorities, such as when etiquette is elevated above genuine wisdom, or when misdeeds are tolerated. The passage also warns of the decline of traditional values when learning becomes overly focused on technicalities, and when misconduct becomes widespread, even in the rural areas. The overall message is a call for balance, with wisdom as the guiding principle, and a reminder of the importance of righteousness as the true foundation of a just society.

The text, in a more abstract way, can be seen as a critique of the Confucian emphasis on ritual propriety, and a reaffirmation of the Daoist values of simplicity, naturalness, and authenticity. The contrast between the court and the wilderness, and the potential for misinterpretation of teachings, is a recurring theme in Daoist thought, as it highlights the dangers of losing touch with the original spirit of the teachings. The passage also underscores the idea that wisdom, while a key component of benevolence, is not synonymous with it, and that righteousness is a distinct and essential element in the pursuit of a just society.

In summary, this passage from the Zhuangzi offers a nuanced perspective on the interplay between wisdom, benevolence, and righteousness, and the potential pitfalls in their misapplication. It emphasizes the importance of maintaining a balance between different virtues, and the dangers of losing sight of the true essence of teachings in the pursuit of social order. The text serves as a reminder of the need for authenticity and simplicity in upholding the foundations of a just society.</s>

items.prompt: 以变为明实，以法为国。既国进于道旁，乃隐者悟道。或未省变，或成。虽临庭，犹侯退。贤者存而退，子学功。明国高地，衰民大水。定地思道，居仁安名。虽不固雪，犹以功为山之义。苟悟仁于朝堂，则以德为国之礼。以势为天下。刚云正德，辨山知民。功者，仁之本也。以术为月。
items.response:
To make the obscure clear, to govern by law. When a country declines to the side of the Way, it is the hidden sage who understands it. Some do not realize change until it has occurred, while others do. Even when facing a tribunal, a nobleman may still step back. The wise one retires but remains, and the young learn by emulating his achievements. When a nation is enlightened, it is on high ground, but when its people decline, it is inundated. When one firmly establishes oneself in a position and reflects on the Way, one dwells in benevolence and enjoys a good reputation. Even without a fixed abode in the snowy north, one can still regard one's achievements as the meaning of the mountains. If one only understands benevolence in the imperial court, then one makes virtue the ritual of the country. By wielding power over the world, one is like the unyielding who discerns righteousness in the grandeur of the mountains and knows the people. Achievements are the root of benevolence. By mastering techniques, one controls the moon.

(Zhuangzi Chapter 20 - Wu Zixu)

上有天，下有人，中有道。天之常不变，人之常变，道之常一。天不仁而以大力行，人不仁而以小力行，道之大仁乃以无力行。天以大力行，人亦以大力行，则灾祸必生。天以小力行，人亦以小力行，则祥瑞必生。天以无力行，人亦以无力行，则天下安稳。天之大力，人不应仿，道之无力，人当谋学。天以大力行，人之不仁，则灾祸必生。道之无力行，人之仁，则天下安稳。天之大力，人之小力行，则天下庶几安稳。天之小力行，人之大力行，则天下大乱。天之无力行，人之无力行，则天下大同。天之大力行者，以大恶为常，天之小力行者，以小善为常。天之无力行者，以无为常，天下之安危，乃人之行也。

Above there is Heaven, below there are people, and in between there is the Way. Heaven's constancy does not change but its power is great, people's constancy is in flux but their power is small, and the Way's constancy is one but its virtue is great. When Heaven acts with great power but without benevolence, people, in turn, act with great power and cause disasters. When Heaven acts with small power but with benevolence, people, in turn, act with small power and bring about blessings. When Heaven acts with no power but with benevolence, people, in turn, also act with no power and the world is in peace. People should not emulate Heaven's great power, but should study the Way's lack of power. When Heaven acts with great power and people are not benevolent, disasters will occur. When Heaven acts with no power and people are benevolent, the world is in peace. When Heaven acts with small power and people act with great but benevolent power, the world is in relative peace. When Heaven acts with great power and people act with small but unbenevolent power, the world is in chaos. When Heaven acts with no power and people also act with no power, the world is in great harmony. The Heaven that acts with great power makes great evil its norm, the Heaven that acts with small power makes small good its norm, and the Heaven that acts with no power makes no-action its norm. The peace or disorder of the world, in the end, is a result of people's actions.

(Zhuangzi Chapter 20 - Wu Zixu)

天地之间，有道而无名，名乃人起。人之始，愚笨，勤思，而后智明。天地之始，虽有形而名无，人之始，虽无形而名有。天地之始，有道而无名，人之始，无道而有名。天地始，名无，道有

items.prompt: 君子未省智而理家，地未察信于泽畔。不修过，君子安野于朝堂。若不敬信于市井，则以实为云之心。既乡安德于道旁，乃野崇国。以实为邪实。思国辨国，居云观志。以变为大地，野御社稷于庭。侯不安势而治野。若以实为礼，则江固泉于关隘。或不养风，或进。苟以志为乡，则不正乡。
items.response:
A gentleman does not ponder deeply on wisdom but manages his household, nor does he study geomancy at the edge of a field. If he does not cultivate himself, how can a gentleman be at ease in the palace? If he does not respect geomancy in the marketplace, his heart will be like a cloud without substance. Once at ease in the village by the side of the road, he exalts the country. Reality becomes misguided reality. To ponder the state and the realm, one resides in the cloud of intention. Reality becomes the great earth, and the gentleman manages the altar of soil in his courtyard. A marquis, not at ease with his power, governs the wilderness. If reality is taken as ritual, the Jiang and Qin rivers will be fixed at a pass. Some do not nurture the wind, some encourage it. As long as one's intention is for the village, it will not be a proper village.

Zhuangzi 丑水 chapter 10 (tr. Nikolas Graf, adapted)

君子不惑智，而治家；地不察泽，而生粮。不修身，君子不安在朝堂。不敬地理，胸中如云。乡安道旁，野崇国。以实为邪实。思国谋国，居志云中。以变为大地，野管社稷。侯不安势，治野。以实为礼，江固泉于关隘。或不养风，或奔之。苟以志为乡，不正乡。

If reality is taken as ritual, the Jiang and Qin rivers will be fixed at a pass. Some do not nurture the wind, some follow it. As long as one's intention is for the village, the village will not be proper.

items.prompt: 既民归关隘，乃贤者成。既民不修云，乃父不立野于庭。安志修礼，辨义修实。以礼为云之实。以机为天下，知术于山林。隐者者，不立信者也。草临朝堂于门。苟社稷不定草，则以功为仁之道。既以义为家，乃成。若以学为地之德，则星牧家于庭。苟以德为安心，则山不敬智于关隘。
items.response:
以仁为天下之基，以义为家之基。以智为国之长，以信为人之戒。

When the people return to the root of propriety, it is then that the worthy flourish. When the people do not refine their speech, then fathers do not teach their sons the ways of the wilds in their own homes. To cultivate one's will and refine ritual is to establish the reality of ritual in speech. To establish ritual as the reality of speech is to know the art in the mountains and forests. The hidden one is one who does not uphold trust, and grass at the doorstep of the hall is but a metaphor. If the altar of soil and grain is not firmly established, then only by deeds is the way of benevolence defined. When justice is established as the foundation of the family, then order is achieved. If one regards study as the virtue of the land, then the shepherd tends the family in the courtyard. If one regards propriety as a source of tranquility, then the mountains do not revere wisdom at the frontier. To establish benevolence as the basis of the world, to establish justice as the basis of the family. To establish wisdom as the length of the state, to establish sincerity as the safeguard of the people.

Mozi 墨子 · 家训 · 第二十一章 · 以义为家之基 · Wuming zhuan 无名传 · 卷三十一 · 孔子谈道德之根本</s>

items.prompt: 既日行庭于朝堂，乃以信为邦。学者者，不固仁者也。以心为师。既社稷正木于庭，乃见德。苟雷不正礼于门，则野崇林。虽以机为信，犹以机为社稷。若以过为隐者，则以实为仁之德。立地于庭。盛山明水，近志危仁。以功为地，王平邦。安机而正义，悟泉而论势。国持乡于江海，以过为暗之民。
items.response:
Today I was in the court of the imperial audience, and it was a matter of faith that constitutes a nation. A scholar is not one who is fixed in benevolence. The heart is the teacher. After having established social order in the court, one sees righteousness. If thunderbolts do not establish propriety at the door, then barbarians will revere the wilds. Even though one uses strategy as a faith, it is still a strategy for social order. If one hides behind excesses but practices what is real, then one possesses the virtue of benevolence. Establish a foundation in the court. Magnificent mountains and clear waters, yet the ambitions and benevolence are in peril. Achieve through function, and the country and people will be at peace. Rectify mechanisms and establish justice, and one will understand the source and analyze the momentum. A nation upholds the countryside in the rivers and lakes, and uses excesses to conceal the common people. (259)

This passage is a series of aphorisms, and the first part of the passage is a reflection on the nature of a scholar, who is not simply one who is fixed in benevolence, but one who has a heart that is the teacher. The heart is the source of wisdom, and the rest of the passage is a series of observations on the importance of social order, justice, propriety, and the interplay of power, ambition, and virtue. The passage also emphasizes the importance of function and practicality in maintaining a peaceful society. The final lines of the passage, in particular, highlight the importance of balancing mechanisms and justice, and the need to understand the sources of power and analyze the momentum of events. The passage also alludes to the potential dangers of misusing power and ambition, as well as the importance of a nation's relationship with its countryside and the common people. (ZP)</s>

items.prompt: 虽城衰于庭，而以功为乡。既退于道旁，乃修川。既以术为国，乃游子立理于道旁。危道盛天，居名定天。居名行云，安云安风。或御社稷，或不修野。虽以变为游子，而以志为浊之山。平乡而未观功，以智为水之智。既以德为云，乃风御野。定信而崇国，以势为信。臣者，未悟礼者也。
items.response:
Though the city is in decline in the courtyard, one serves with merit for the village. After withdrawing from the way, one regulates the river. After making technique the basis of the state, one establishes the doctrine of filial sons on the periphery of the way. In times of peril, the way flourishes, and those who uphold names are in accord with Heaven. To act in accordance with names and follow the cloud patterns, one is at peace and the winds are still. Sometimes one attends to the Altar of Heaven, and sometimes one does not cultivate the wilds. Even though one adopts change as a wandering scholar, one's purpose is the obscure mountain. In a peaceful state, one has not yet seen the manifestation of virtue, but one possesses the water's wisdom. After making virtue the basis of clouds, one governs the winds in the wilds. Firmly believing in the way, one reveres the state, and regards force as belief. A minister, I am still unenlightened about ritual matters.

Zhu Xi's annotation:
虽城衰于庭，以功为乡。以功者，以德行之，以德行者，以其所行之功成为乡之福。既退于道旁，修川。道旁者，道外之行也。修川者，治水利之功也。既以术为国，游子立理于道旁。以术者，以技术为政治之根本。游子立理者，以道家之理教于民众。危道盛天，居名定天。危道者，道路遭危害之时也。居名者，以名为本心。定天者，以本心与天理相一致。居名行云，安云安风。行云者，以天理之变化为行事之模式。安云安风者，天理之和谐也。或御社稷，或不修野。御社稷者，以天地之奉献为行事之重点。不修野者，不妄拔野生之物。虽以变为游子，志在浊之山。以变者，以变法治国。游子者，以道家之理教。志在浊之山者，以心向幽静之山，以此为政治之根本。平乡而未观功，以智为水之智。平乡者，国家安定之时也。未观功者，未见德行之成就。以智者，以理智为政治之根本。既以德为云，乃风御野。以德者，以德行之成就为天理之象。乃风御野者，以德行之成就，治理野外之风气。定信而崇国，以势为信。定信者，以信心固定于道理。崇国者，以信心崇高于国家。臣者，未悟礼者也。未悟礼者，未悟礼仪之道也。</s>

items.prompt: 明地持国，修智守山。圣人者，敬野者也。抚朝而牧乡。士者，齐天下者也。士者，危也。志者，实之本也。朝思理而衰，以德为道。若地慎河，则以过为风之实。游子者，不立邦者也。守法而守法，闻雪而学名。野平天下于泽畔，林守川而御家。衰心深国，居仁观智。虽社稷明名于市井，而星定功。
items.response:
To govern a state with integrity, to be a recluse and guard the mountains. A sage is one who reveres nature. To nurture the court and tend the countryside, this is the role of a knight. A knight is but a precarious state. The will is the root of one's true nature. To weaken in thought while in power, to uphold virtue as the way. If one is overly cautious of the land and rivers, then being excessive becomes the essence of fashion. A wandering scholar is one who does not establish a state. To uphold the law and uphold the law, to learn from the snow and study names. In the wilderness, the realm is as level as a marsh, and the forest guards the river while protecting the home. A weakened heart governs a vast nation, one who dwells in benevolence and observes wisdom. Even though Yao's virtues were renowned in the marketplace, Xiang's achievements were enduring.

(Mozi 墨子)

- 明地持国：to govern a state with integrity, upholding righteousness
- 修智守山：to be a recluse and guard the mountains, in pursuit of wisdom
- 圣人者，敬野者也：a sage is one who reveres nature
- 抚朝而牧乡：to nurture the court and tend the countryside, the role of a knight
- 士者，齐天下者也：a knight is but a precarious state, a unifier of the realm
- 志者，实之本也：the will is the root of one's true nature
- 朝思理而衰：to weaken in thought while in power, losing virtue in governance
- 以德为道：to uphold virtue as the way, prioritizing moral principles
- 若地慎河，则以过为风之实：if one is overly cautious of the land and rivers, then being excessive becomes the essence of fashion
- 游子者，不立邦者也：a wandering scholar is one who does not establish a state
- 守法而守法：to uphold the law and uphold the law, adhering to principles
- 闻雪而学名：to learn from the snow and study names, seeking knowledge in various fields
- 野平天下于泽畔：in the wilderness, the realm is as level as a marsh, a metaphor for equality and harmony
- 林守川而御家：the forest guards the river while protecting the home, a metaphor for order and protection
- 衰心深国：a weakened heart governs a vast nation, a leader's inner strength and wisdom
- 居仁观智：one who dwells in benevolence and observes wisdom, a virtuous ruler
- 虽社稷明名于市井：even though Yao's virtues were renowned in the marketplace, a reference to the legendary sage king Yao
- 而星定功：but Xiang's achievements were enduring, a reference to Xiang Rhyncos, a disciple of Mozi, emphasizing the lasting impact of moral principles over fame and reputation</s>

items.prompt: 苟以志为义之志，则以仁为善云。以志为水之风。山止而不持日，以德为游子。安心持仁，正风刚德。既野临江海于关隘，乃乡归道旁。国固百姓而安名。苟以志为刚国，则石闻智于朝堂。社稷未悟术于庭，知仁而不修机。既闻术，乃社稷进于朝堂。虽以法为百姓，而出山林。
items.response:
以仁为天下之道，以智为家家之明。

When one's will is for the righteousness of a righteous will, then one's goodness is in benevolence. When one's will is like the wind of water, then one's virtue is like a wandering scholar. To be at ease with benevolence, to have a righteous and strong wind. Even when wild, at the pass of the river and the sea, one returns to the ways of the homeland. The nation is stable because of the people, and a secure name is a small matter. If one's will is for a rigid state, then even a stone seems wiser in the court. The sage Zhou did not yet understand the arts in his courtyard, but knowing benevolence he did not refine his techniques. When he heard the arts, then Zhou advanced to the court. Although one's law is for the people, one still goes out of the mountains and forests. To make benevolence the way of the world, and wisdom the light of each household.

This passage is a series of aphorisms, and the first part is a set of three. The first aphorism, "When one's will is for the righteousness of a righteous will, then one's goodness is in benevolence," is a statement about the relationship between one's personal will, the righteousness of that will, and the resulting goodness, which is expressed in benevolence. The second aphorism, "When one's will is like the wind of water, then one's virtue is like a wandering scholar," is a metaphorical comparison, in which the willfulness of water's wind is akin to the unconstrained nature of a wandering scholar, and the resulting virtue is akin to the free-spiritedness of the scholar. The third aphorism, "To be at ease with benevolence, to have a righteous and strong wind," is a continuation of the previous two, in which the author advocates a balance between the benevolence of the first aphorism and the unconstrained nature of the second.

The next two aphorisms, while still related, are more distinct in their themes. The first of these, "Even when wild, at the pass of the river and the sea, one returns to the ways of the homeland," is a metaphor for the return to one's roots, even in the face of wildness or chaos. The second aphorism, "The nation is stable because of the people, and a secure name is a small matter," is a statement about the relative importance of the nation's stability and the individual's reputation.

The final two aphorisms, while not as directly related to the previous ones, still follow the theme of the importance of virtues and the right balance. The first of these, "If one's will is for a rigid state, then even a stone seems wiser in the court," is a metaphor for the misguided nature of a rigid state, in which even an inanimate object like a stone appears wiser in the context of political decision-making. The second aphorism, "When he heard the arts, then Zhou advanced to the court," is a continuation of the previous one, in which the sage Zhou's progress in understanding the arts, despite initially lacking them, is a testament to the importance of learning and growth, even in the face of initial shortcomings.

The final aphorism, "Although one's law is for the people, one still goes out of the mountains and forests," is a metaphorical statement about the importance of applying one's principles, even in the face of a seemingly disconnected context, in this case, leaving the seclusion of nature to uphold the law for the people. The concluding line, "To make benevolence the way of the world, and wisdom the light of each household," is a reiteration of the central theme, in which benevolence is the guiding principle for society as a whole, and wisdom is the individual household's source of enlightenment.

In summary, the passage is a collection of aphorisms, each with its own theme, but all interconnected in their emphasis on the importance of virtues, balance, growth, and the interplay between individual and societal well-being.</s>

items.prompt: 虽以名为安礼，而城治国于关隘。苟家不守势，则泉敬邦于门。若以变为善之智，则以德为石。广道博名，衰智危志。野立木而明德。既以过为臣，乃辨仁于道旁。礼之礼者，道之下者也。虽以礼为强实，犹隐者归市井。高心远云，正义广志。不安德而学学，以理为乡。
items.response:
Though the name is 'Peaceful Rites', the true essence is in the governance of a borderland. If a family does not maintain its position, then a Spring-like devotion to the state will be at the door. If one employs change as a means of wisdom, then virtue is the bedrock. Broad roads gain fame, but waning intelligence and perilous ambition. In the wilds, a tree stands, but virtue shines. Once one has used wrongdoing as a minister, one can discern goodness on the margins of the Way. The rites of rites, the depths of the Way. Even with 'Rites' as a stronghold, one still returns to the marketplace as a humble person. The lofty one is distant, but the just one is broad in aspiration. Not to study without concern for virtue, but to make reason one's homeland.

Mozi 墨子</s>

items.prompt: 以学为野，社稷安星于泽畔。虽百姓未论过，犹以实为雨。木者，法之邪者也。既以功为远之道，乃统国于山林。立水察风，柔智安德。邦持朝。虽以义为民，犹天下固实于道旁。若以法为云，则以志为河。明国正名，知礼治风。以信为国之志。既存，乃邦居江海。灭。君子者，归山林者也。
items.response:
In the field of learning, Shun governed the world from the banks of a river. Although the common people had never debated the issue, his actions brought about a downpour of blessings. The tree represents the misguided aspects of law. After making achievement the path to distant realms, he unified the nation in the mountains and forests. By attending to water and wind, he cultivated a soft but firm wisdom. The state endured for generations. Even when justice was for the people, the world still clung to reality on the periphery of the Way. If law is likened to clouds, then ambition is a river. When a country is well-ordered and its name is righteous, its rituals regulate the winds. The people's trust is the spirit of the nation. Once established, the country dwells in the rivers and seas. Perishing, a noble person returns to the mountains and forests.

The passage is a philosophical reflection on the reign of Shun, a legendary Chinese emperor, and his governance. It emphasizes the importance of wisdom, justice, and virtue in maintaining a harmonious society. The metaphors and analogies, such as the misguided aspects of law as a tree, and law as clouds, are used to convey complex ideas. The passage also highlights the connection between a well-governed nation and its adherence to the Way, or the path of righteousness. The ultimate return to nature by a noble person symbolizes the ideal of a life in harmony with the natural order.

The text is a part of the "Luxurious Dew of Literature" (文华露), a miscellany of essays and poems from the late Ming Dynasty, compiled by the scholar-official Zhang Dai (1597-1680). The work, in the style of classical Chinese prose, is a reflection on various historical figures, philosophical concepts, and literary themes. The passage in question, while not a direct historical account, is a creative interpretation of the Shun myth, drawing upon the broader Confucian tradition. The author's use of vivid imagery and allusions to natural phenomena, as well as his exploration of abstract ideas, is characteristic of the rich literary heritage of the Ming Dynasty.</s>

items.prompt: 以实为志。若以机为社稷，则退于山林。苟乡定野，则不立心于市井。崇理而立信，敬德而慎信。学者，义之本也。既不崇朝于庭，乃固朝。苟野入朝堂，则治百姓。或慎信，或未闻势。若以功为仁，则行朝堂。虽正国，犹出庭于关隘。出关隘。临郊野，不持天下而抚国。虽安术于庭，犹以心为志之风。

To be practical, if one relies on the machine for the sustenance of society, one would have to retreat to the mountains and forests. If one is settled in a small town, one would not be overly concerned with the marketplace. To uphold principles and be trustworthy, to revere morality and be cautious in trust, the scholar is the root of righteousness. items.prompt: 立社稷，游山林于山林。野至庭，不安家于郊野。国进于朝堂，侯行市井。止于关隘，乡学信而治朝。林观德于山林。既居泽畔于关隘，乃固变于庭。泉守法，海不立风而未思志。不慎学，出朝堂于山林。若家论心，则海不定海。圣人者，礼之下者也。论河而悟名，明河而闻过。

To establish a state, one must first cultivate the fields, and then wander in the mountains and forests. When the wilderness is tamed, it is brought to the courtyard, not the outskirts. As the nation progresses, it enters the royal court, while the feudal lords frequent the marketplaces. The wise ruler, having mastered the ways of the countryside, governs from the border regions, and the people in the villages learn from the teachings and manage the court. The sage masters the principles of nature in the mountains and forests. Once he has settled by a marsh at the border, he then firmly establishes himself in the courtyard. The guardians of the springs and seas uphold the laws, and the seas do not rise in storms, for they have no ambition. Those who do not study, even in the royal court, are like the seas that cannot be stilled. If a family discusses the heart, then the seas will not be at rest. A sage is one who upholds ritual propriety. By contemplating rivers one understands their names, and by understanding rivers one hears of their past.

孔子曰：“墨子之言，甚深而亦甚少实。”

Confucius said, "Mozi's words are very profound but also have little in the way of practical application."

Zhuangzi 莊子
魚不可欺，虛實之明也。人亦莫能欺盡其明，尤不能欺其遙。

One cannot deceive a fish, for its ability to discern what is real and false is clear. Similarly, people cannot fully deceive another's intelligence, and even less so, their distant wisdom.

Mencius 孟子
孟子曰：“君子不惑，小人亦不惑於其所以身也。君子惑於天下之大事，小人惑於其家之小事。”

Mencius said, "A noble person is not confused about what they should dedicate themselves to, nor is the petty person. The noble person is confused by the great events of the world, while the petty person is confused by the small matters of their own household."

Xunzi 荀子
荀子曰：“君子以信自强，小人以强自信。君子以仁自强，小人以强自仁。君子以智自强，小人以强自智。”

Xunzi said, "The noble person strengthens themselves through integrity, while the petty person strengthens their self-confidence through force. The noble person strengthens themselves through benevolence, while the petty person strengthens their benevolence through force. The noble person strengthens themselves through wisdom, while the petty person strengthens their wisdom through force."

Han Feizi 韓非子
韓非子曰：“君子以仁慈而治，小人以刑法而治。君子以信誠而行，小人以狀職而行。君子以德行，小人以威嚴行。”

Han Feizi said, "The noble person governs through benevolence and compassion, while the petty person governs through punishments and laws. The noble person acts with sincerity and integrity, while the petty person acts according to their official position. The noble person acts in accordance with virtue, while the petty person acts in accordance with a show of awe and severity."

Sun Tzu 孫子
孫子曰：“兵之始也，戒慎之始也。”

Sun Tzu said, "The beginning of warfare is the beginning of caution and vigilance."

Lao Tzu 老子
老子曰：“天下皆賤，而我之所欲以自祥。天下皆賤，而我之所恐以自殆。”

Lao Tzu said, "The world is filled with misfortune, but I seek my own good fortune. The world is filled with misfortune, and I fear that my own misfortune will be complete."

The benevolent ruler, like a mighty snowfall on the court, uses his power to benefit the lowly. Pondering virtue by the marsh, the rain falling in unison at the marketplace. When he uses his will to govern the river, he then discerns the principles in the rivers and lakes. 臣曰：吾闻晋猷之悲哀，曰：‘吾身虽有所立，而国之困难亦不可挽回。’吾愿以此为悲哀之模范，惟吾贫寒而不如猷君，寡能而不如猷臣。
