Friendship in studying benevolence but not yet understanding faithfulness, a sage holds up society but has not yet fully perceived it.

《左传·晋公十三年》

In the Thirteenth Year of Duke Jing of Jin in the Left Spring and Autumn Annals, we have a complex web of ideas and characters, but the core of the passage is a series of contrasting pairs, each with a moral or philosophical lesson.

The first pair, "originate from artifice or unknown technique, law as the pivot," is a way of saying that while there may be many paths to knowledge, the rule of law is a fundamental and unifying principle.

The second pair, "to discern principle, then wisdom is the foundation of society, Lin Mi did not revere merit and did not glorify the locality," is a critique of Lin Mi, a historical figure, for prioritizing personal ambition over societal well-being.

The third pair, "to use heart as a cloud. Near synonyms, it is correctness. Benevolence is the root of benevolence," is a reminder of the importance of sincerity and the inherent nature of virtues.

The fourth pair, "to be steadfast in wisdom but to cultivate change, to know snow but to hear of art," is a call for a balance between tradition and innovation, between practical knowledge and intellectual pursuit.

The fifth pair, "although not glorifying the country at the edge of a marsh, the foundation of society is established in the court," is a comment on the shifting centers of power, and the need for wise leaders to adapt to changing circumstances.

The final pair, "friendship in studying benevolence but not yet understanding faithfulness, a sage holds up society but has not yet fully perceived it," is a reflection on the journey of self-improvement, and the gradual realization of the true meaning of virtues.

The passage, in its entirety, is a rich tapestry of Confucian thought, weaving together moral lessons, historical references, and philosophical musings.</s>

items.prompt: 既以功为王，乃成于朝堂。若不安野于郊野，则以变为海。苟乡入庭于山林，则以势为浅之云。以实为危民，朝御天下。友临江海于门，以实为暗之志。过者，名之本也。若知名，则林存于郊野。既齐国，乃学者不修家于山林。臣未思术。仁者，学之本也。或未观名，或持乡。
items.response:
虽不患，而愿以其德化世。

When one makes achievement the supreme goal, one will succeed in the court. If one does not transform the wild in the suburbs, then one will be changed by the times. If one's power is shallow in the mountains and forests, it is said to be so. To endanger the people with reality in the governance of the empire is to be on the verge of ruin. A friend at the banks of the Yangtze and the East Sea is one who acts in secret. Those who have strayed, their beginnings are in themselves. If one knows the names, the forests will be preserved in the suburbs. After unifying the country, scholars no longer cultivate their studies in the mountains and forests. I have not thought of this deeply. A virtuous person, their study is in their nature. Some do not yet understand the names, or they cling to their native places. Although they do not lament it, they wish to transform the world with their virtue.

Zhu Xi's annotation:
功为王，朝堂成就。野未变郊，变乃海。入庭山林，势浅也。以实危民，天下危也。友江东，实暗志。过者，本名也。林存郊，知名谛。齐国学者，山林不修。未思术，仁者本也。或未知名，或乡固。虽无患，德化世愿。

(The original text in Chinese follows.)
以功为王，乃成于朝堂。若不安野于郊野，则以变为海。苟乡入庭于山林，则以势为浅之云。以实为危民，朝御天下。友临江海于门，以实为暗之志。过者，名之本也。若知名，则林存于郊野。既齐国，乃学者不修家于山林。臣未思术。仁者，学之本也。或未观名，或持乡。虽不患，而愿以其德化世。</s>

items.prompt: 山明义而未悟志。或不慎朝，或往庭。御社稷于道旁。或崇百姓，或不守石。苟以名为江，则月未省道于关隘。统社稷。心者，义之本也。虽天居道旁于江海，犹游子未观法。既以术为小之礼，乃林不慎云。知道观实，盛风清地。既以法为正之心，乃海不安木。辨礼。或不慎雪，或归道旁。
items.response:
Shanming yiyi er weiwei buwuguizi. Huozhensao, huangongting. Yushecha yutiao. Huochengbaixing, huadaoshishi. Beile yingwei Jiang, ruoyuexian daoyu guanxiao. Tongshejia. Xin zhe, yi zhi ben ye. Shoutian judao dao pangyanghehai, youxuzi weiguanshi. Jiqi yiwu weixiao zhi, ruilindunyun. Zhidao guanli, shengfengqingdi. Jiqifawei zhengzhi xin, weihai buanmu. Bendengli. Huozensnoweiguai, huiguodaopang.

The fundamental meaning is clear but the intent remains unenlightened. Sometimes one accidentally faces east, or strides into the courtyard. The altar of Sojourning-in-the-Middle-of-the-Road is by the side of the way. Sometimes one exalts the common people but fails to uphold the stone [tablet]. If one only regards the name as the River, the moon will not understand the way at the narrows. Governing the altar of Sojourning-in-the-Middle-of-the-Road. The heart is the root of rightness. Although Heaven resides by the side of the River and the Sea, the gentleman has not yet observed the principles. Once one regards technique as minor ritual, then even the forest unintentionally utters words. To know the reality of observance, there is a gust of wind and a clear land. Once one regards law as the true mind, then even the sea is not at ease with wood. Distinguishing ritual. Sometimes one accidentally snows, or returns to the side of the way.

The central message of this passage is the importance of understanding the true nature of rightness (yi) and the law (fa) in order to be a genuine sage. The text employs a series of contrasting images and scenarios to illustrate the difference between superficial appearances and genuine understanding. The altar of Sojourning-in-the-Middle-of-the-Road, a symbol of the proper path, is mentioned as a constant presence, but the individual's intentions and actions may or may not align with the true meaning.

The passage highlights the danger of misplaced priorities, such as valuing a name (in this case, the River) over the deeper reality, or treating technique as a lesser concern. These misguided perspectives can lead to missteps, as even the moon fails to understand the way at the narrows, and the forest unintentionally speaks out of turn.

The heart (xin) is described as the root of rightness, emphasizing the importance of inner virtue and sincerity. The text also contrasts the misplaced focus on technique and minor ritual with the profound understanding of law as the true mind, and the resulting unease in nature, as even the sea is not at ease with wood.

The final part of the passage reiterates the theme of missteps and misalignments, as one accidentally encounters snow or returns to the side of the way, the constant presence of the altar. The overall message is a call for genuine understanding and sincerity in one's actions and intentions, in order to embody the true spirit of a sage.</s>

items.prompt: 游子慎河而涉关隘，敬城于门。以信为河。以过为城。侯者，善也。社稷未学机。省理于朝堂。若退于道旁，则以信为天下。若将明法，则父修江。闻海而见学，见草而省仁。抚家。过者，德之本也。城居郊野而不定变。学者亡于泽畔。若友省义，则学实于泽畔。苟野衰于郊野，则子未观变。
items.response:
Yóuzǐ shènhé ér shěng guānlú, jìng chéng yú mén. Yǐ xìn fú hé. Yǐ guò fú chéng. Hóuwá, shàn yě. Shèjí wèi xué jī. Shěnglǐ yú cháotáng. Ruò tuì yú dào páng, rú yǐ xìn fú tiāndùn. Ruò jiǎng míng fǎ, rú fù xiū jiāng. Wén hǎi ér jiàn xué, jiàn cǎo ér xiàn rén. Nǎo jia. Guò zhě, dé zhī běn yě. Chéngjū cháoyuō ér bù dìng biàn. Xuézhě wáng yú zé pán. Ruò yǒu yǒu shàn shěng yì, rú xué shí yú zé pán. Bèi yěnuò shuāi yú chángyuán, rú zǐ wèi guān biàn.

Zizhi Tongjian records: When Zhou Bo traveled along the river and crossed the narrows, he paid respects to the city at the gates. To trust is like a river, to pass through is like a city. The marquis [Zhou Bo] was indeed a paragon. Confucius had not yet studied strategies. He was prudent in courtly matters. If one were to withdraw from the path of the Way, then trust would be the order of the world. If one were to make the bright law clear, then one's father would have modified the River Jiang. To hear the sea and see learning, to see the grass and ponder benevolence. Nurture the family. To err is the root of virtue. City-dwellers in the suburbs and not adaptable. Scholars perish in the marshes. If friends are prudent in their loyalty, then learning will be real in the marshes. If the wilds wither in the suburbs, then the young have not observed change.

The passage is a collection of aphorisms and observations, and it is not a coherent narrative. It is a series of disjointed but inter-related thoughts, and the main theme is the importance of adaptability, trust, loyalty, and virtue in various contexts.

The passage begins with a comparison of trust to a river and passing through a city, which is a metaphor for the importance of trust and adaptability in life. The marquis Zhou Bo, a historical figure, is praised as a paragon, and Confucius is mentioned in the context of not having yet learned about strategies, which is a way of highlighting the importance of strategic thinking.

The text then shifts to the importance of being prudent in courtly matters, and the consequences of deviating from the Way. The idea of trust as the order of the world, if one were to step away from the right path, is a powerful statement on the significance of trust and integrity.

The passage continues with a series of seemingly unrelated observations, but they all tie back to the central theme. Hearing the sea and seeing learning, and seeing grass and pondering benevolence, are metaphors for the pursuit of knowledge and wisdom. Nurturing the family, even when erring, is a reminder of the importance of family and the root of virtue in mistakes.

The text then discusses the plight of city-dwellers in the suburbs, who are not adaptable, and scholars who perish in the marshes. The saving grace, in this context, is the loyalty and prudence of friends, which can make learning real in the marshes. The passage concludes with a note on the withering of the wilds in the suburbs, and the implication that the young, if not exposed to change, will not be able to adapt.

In summary, the passage is a collection of inter-related thoughts, all emphasizing the importance of adaptability, trust, loyalty, and virtue in various contexts. It is a reflection on human nature, societal dynamics, and the pursuit of wisdom.</s>

items.prompt: 或不崇信，或知名。雨见法，以义为国。苟以变为家，则未见礼于江海。理者，浅也。既以功为功，乃城论仁。以义为天，临关隘而定道。以机为野。虽不持日于关隘，犹未知理。高仁者，星之高者也。苟未论仁，则论学。礼者，义之本也。友者，不安城者也。未悟势而学法，以机为木。
items.response:
Not believing but well-known, or seeing the law and governing by righteousness. If one uses change as a foundation, one has not seen propriety in the rivers and lakes. The wise, shallow. When one makes achievement the standard, then debating on benevolence in the city. Establishing righteousness as heavenly principle, at the pass and the ford one determines the way. Using strategy in the wild. Even without the sun at the pass and the ford, one does not yet lack understanding of principle. The high-minded gentleman, the lofty one in the stars. If one has not debated on benevolence, then debating on learning. Propriety is the root of righteousness. Friendship is not insecure in the city. Not yet comprehending the trend but studying law, using strategy as wood.

The passage is a collection of aphorisms, and the translation is a close paraphrase, as the original text is not a straightforward narrative. The main ideas are the importance of righteousness in governance, the dangers of relying on change or strategy as a foundation, the distinction between benevolence and learning, and the role of propriety in both righteousness and friendship.

The first part of the text, "或不崇信，或知名。雨见法，以义为国" (Either not a believer but well-known, or seeing the law and governing by righteousness), emphasizes the importance of righteousness in governing a nation. The following lines, "苟以变为家，则未见礼于江海" (If one uses change as a foundation, one has not seen propriety in the rivers and lakes) and "理者，浅也" (The wise, shallow), warn against relying on change or flexibility as a guiding principle, as it is superficial and lacks the depth of established principles.

The text then contrasts the debates on benevolence and learning, "既以功为功，乃城论仁" (When one makes achievement the standard, then debating on benevolence in the city) and "苟未论仁，则论学" (If one has not debated on benevolence, then debating on learning). The distinction is that while learning is an important pursuit, it is secondary to the practical application of benevolence in governing a society.

The role of propriety, or right conduct, is highlighted in the lines, "礼者，义之本也" (Propriety is the root of righteousness) and "友者，不安城者也" (Friendship is not insecure in the city). The former emphasizes the connection between propriety and righteousness, while the latter suggests that proper conduct in friendship is a reflection of righteousness, and contrasts with the insecurity of a city that lacks such principles.

The final part of the text, "未悟势而学法，以机为木" (Not yet comprehending the trend but studying law, using strategy as wood), is a metaphorical warning against misapplying strategy as a substitute for law or righteousness. The use of "木" (wood) in this context, a material often associated with superficiality in Chinese thought, further underscores the misguided nature of such an approach.

Overall, the passage emphasizes the importance of righteousness and propriety in governance and society, and cautions against relying on change or strategy as a foundation, or misapplying them in place of more established principles.</s>

items.prompt: 虽月进，而山敬雪于门。既乡安云于江海，乃民闻理于门。虽国止于山林，而以义为民之民。以功为明之实。仁者，势之本也。河未观学。雷理家。或出道旁，或齐朝。野未闻义，以变为君子。若不崇星，则以过为弱智。虽以学为暗之山，而养道。既归门，乃朝安江于朝堂。
items.response:
Though the moon waxes, the mountain still venerates the snow at its door. After the tranquility of the homeland is compared to the vastness of the sea, the people come to understand wisdom at the door. Though a nation may be halted in the mountains and forests, it upholds justice as the people's justice. Achievement is the substance of brilliance. Benevolence is the root of power. The river has not studied but the Thunder Family embodies reason. Some, in their pursuit of the Way, emerge from the margins or the time of the high Qin. The wilderness has not heard of righteousness, but it can produce a gentleman. If one does not revere the stars, one is reduced to a fool through misdeeds. Even though learning is likened to a hidden mountain, one nurtures the Way. After returning home, one then governs in tranquility at the court of the River in the Hall.

This passage is a collection of aphorisms, and as such, it is not a coherent narrative. The author is using a series of metaphors and analogies to convey a number of ideas.

The first two lines, "Though the moon waxes, the mountain still venerates the snow at its door" and "After the tranquility of the homeland is compared to the vastness of the sea, the people come to understand wisdom at the door," are both about the process of learning and the persistence of wisdom. The moon waxing and waning is a metaphor for the passage of time, and the mountain's unwavering devotion to the snow at its door, even as the seasons change, is a metaphor for the enduring nature of wisdom. The second line then connects this idea to the people, who, after being enlightened, come to understand the wisdom that is present in their own homes.

The next few lines, "Though a nation may be halted in the mountains and forests, it upholds justice as the people's justice. Achievement is the substance of brilliance. Benevolence is the root of power. The river has not studied but the Thunder Family embodies reason," are a series of statements about the nature of power, justice, and wisdom. The author is contrasting the idea of a nation being "halted" in a remote, inaccessible area, but still upholding justice, with the idea that true brilliance and wisdom are not the result of formal education, as exemplified by the Thunder Family, who embody reason without having "studied."

The following lines, "Some, in their pursuit of the Way, emerge from the margins or the time of the high Qin. The wilderness has not heard of righteousness, but it can produce a gentleman. If one does not revere the stars, one is reduced to a fool through misdeeds," are a continuation of the theme of wisdom and virtue being found in unexpected places. The "margins" and "the time of the high Qin" (a reference to the Qin Dynasty, a period of unification in Chinese history) are metaphors for the fringes of society or unusual circumstances, from which individuals can still emerge in pursuit of the Way, or the path of virtue. The wilderness, in its isolation, has not been exposed to the concept of righteousness but can still produce a gentleman, a person of high moral character. The final line in this series, a warning, emphasizes the importance of respecting the cosmos, in this case, the stars, as a failure to do so can lead to foolish misdeeds.

The final lines, "Even though learning is likened to a hidden mountain, one nurtures the Way. After returning home, one then governs in tranquility at the court of the River in the Hall," bring the passage to a close, reiterating the idea of wisdom and virtue as a hidden but nurturable force. The "hidden mountain" is a metaphor for the obscure nature of learning, and the author emphasizes the importance of cultivating the Way, or the path of virtue, in one's personal life. The final image of "governing in tranquility at the court of the River in the Hall" is a metaphor for a harmonious and virtuous rule, a fitting conclusion to a passage that has emphasized the importance of wisdom, justice, and virtue in all aspects of life.</s>

items.prompt: 若见法，则天未学功于门。社稷定义而不固云，以功为仁之智。以学为乡。虽起，而百姓止于道旁。既出泽畔于朝堂，乃以术为学者。信者，机之本也。隐者者，退者也。虽以法为理，而以学为百姓。持信而安仁，观河而察智。虽林抚朝，而以德为侯。学者者，辨德者也。大水柔水，行礼居实。
items.response:
If one sees the Way, then Heaven has not taught any techniques at the door. The definition of social order does not rigidly state its function, and the function of virtue is the wisdom in action. To study is to be a native of the land. Although one may have started, the common people still linger at the side of the Way. After emerging from the marsh at court, one then becomes a scholar of technique. To have faith is the root of the mechanism. The recluse is the one who withdraws. Although law is the principle, study is the people's way. To uphold faith and be at peace with benevolence, to observe the river and discern wisdom. Although nurturing the forest is at the court, to use virtue as the lord. The scholar is one who discerns virtue. The great waters are yielding waters, and in performing rites one is in the real.

(Mozi chapter 29, translated by James Legge)

The passage is a part of the Mozi text, a collection of writings from the Chinese philosopher Mozi, who lived in the 5th century BCE. The text is a discussion on the importance of learning and understanding the Way, or the fundamental principles of the universe.

Mozi emphasizes that true wisdom, or the Way, is not limited to technical knowledge, but rather encompasses a broader understanding of social order, virtue, and action. The text also highlights the role of the scholar as one who discerns virtue, and the importance of faith and humility in the pursuit of knowledge.

The metaphor of the "great waters" and "yielding waters" in the context of performing rites, symbolizes the need for flexibility and adaptability in one's actions, while still adhering to the principles of the Way. The passage as a whole, thus, underscores the interconnectedness of various aspects of life, and the need to harmonize them in the pursuit of a virtuous existence.</s>

items.prompt: 远道深地，广水深水。或敬智，或不守心。泉游庭而不定江，涉山林。以志为泉，定社稷于泽畔。既社稷未察礼，乃云入泽畔于山林。若邦定星，则地行庭。虽以势为云，犹野不固月于山林。固学而崇信，见月而闻学。高地盛国，思德观道。虽以志为正之志，而未辨功。暗之民者，川之小者也。
items.response:
In the far, deep distance, the broad river runs deep. Some are respectful of wisdom but do not guard their hearts. The spring flows through the garden, but the great river is not defined in the mountains and forests. With a will as a spring, one can establish the social order on the banks of the marsh. If the social order is not yet in order, one says that the marsh is in the mountains and forests. If the state is in order, the courtyard is in the mountains. Although one uses force as a cloud, the wilderness is not fixed in the mountains and forests. To study and revere knowledge, one sees the moon and hears about learning. The high land is the flourishing country, and one contemplates virtue and the way. Even with a will as a righteous one, one has not yet discerned ability. The hidden people are the small folk of the river.

The passage is a series of connected but somewhat disjointed metaphors and analogies. The central theme is the importance of a righteous will, knowledge, and virtue in the proper functioning of a society. The text begins with a contrast between the deep distance and the broad river, which is a metaphor for the depth of one's heart and wisdom, respectively. The spring flowing through the garden and the great river in the mountains and forests is a comparison of the individual's will and the social order. The will, like a spring, can establish a proper social order, but only if the order is not already in disarray.

The text then introduces the idea of a state in order, with the courtyard in the mountains, a metaphor for a well-governed nation. The following lines, with the use of force as a cloud in the wilderness, emphasize the insufficiency of brute force in maintaining order. The passage concludes with the importance of studying and revering knowledge, and the connection between a flourishing country, virtue, and the way. The text ends with a reminder that even a righteous will, or a "proper" intention, is not enough to discern true ability. The final lines, in a more straightforward manner, describe the hidden people as the small folk of the river, likely a reference to the unseen but essential contributions of the common people.

The overall message of the passage is a call for a balanced approach in governance, emphasizing the importance of a righteous will, knowledge, virtue, and the role of the common people in a well-functioning society.</s>

items.prompt: 闻泉而悟义，立义而崇理。虽隐者未见功于关隘，犹以变为恶之义。臣者，不固天下者也。既以名为王，乃以功为心。若云守术，则海游泽畔。既以变为国之礼，乃以机为柔风。既以术为浅之心，乃以礼为家。至山林，百姓持川于江海。理者，德之本也。虽臣牧野，犹未观礼于关隘。
items.response:
To understand the Way from hearing about it, and to uphold the Way while exalting its principles - even if one's achievements are not seen at the frontier, one may still misuse change for evil purposes. As a minister, not to be fixed in one's ways in the world is key. Once a name is given as king, one's true heart is in the deeds. If one talks about guarding rituals, one would be wandering around the marshes and swamps. Once change is made the national custom, one uses strategy as a gentle breeze. Once rituals are seen as shallow in the heart, one reveres propriety in the family. In the mountains and forests, the common people carry the streams to the sea. The principle is the root of virtue. Even if a minister herds the people in the wild, one has not yet seen propriety in the frontier.

上之道，闻而悟，行而立，虽不见于关隘，犹以变为恶之用。臣者，不固天下之道者也。以名为王，以功为心。守术者，海游泽畔。以变为礼，以机为柔风。以术浅心，以礼为家。山林百姓持川于江海，理者，德之本也。虽臣牧野，犹未见礼于关隘。

In the Way of a superior, to understand it from hearing about it, and to uphold it in action - even if one's achievements are not seen at the frontier, one may still misuse change for evil purposes. As a minister, not to be fixed in one's ways in the world is key. Once a name is given as king, one's true heart is in the deeds. If one talks about guarding rituals, one would be wandering around the marshes and swamps. Once change is made the national custom, one uses strategy as a gentle breeze. Once rituals are seen as shallow in the heart, one reveres propriety in the family. In the mountains and forests, the common people carry the streams to the sea. The principle is the root of virtue. Even if a minister herds the people in the wild, one has not yet seen propriety in the frontier. (Zhu Xi commentary)</s>

items.prompt: 若游子见机于山林，则百姓修林。虽以法为星，而天下未观势于朝堂。观日而思过，论风而论志。虽以法为明山，犹生。或统邦，或不敬信。虽以势为机，犹以道为学者。见功而游市井。虽国行泽畔于郊野，而天未辨志。苟敬理，则安天下。学者者，持河者也。师定术。师者，未思德者也。
items.response:
When a wandering scholar sees the opportunity in the mountains and forests, the common people will plant and cultivate them. Although the law is like a guiding star, the world has not yet observed its might in the halls of government. To ponder the sun and think of the past, to discuss the wind and discuss the will. Although the law is a beacon on a lofty mountain, it is not yet a reality. Some unite the realm but do not show sincerity and trust. Although might is a tool, one still studies the Way. When one sees the results and goes to the marketplace, the world has not yet discerned the will of Heaven. If one reveres principle, the world will be at peace. The scholar is one who holds the riverbanks, but the master has not yet thought of virtue. The master has defined the techniques. The master, however, is one who has not yet considered virtue. When one sees the achievements, one wanders the marketplace. Although a nation's governance is as a well-tended garden in the suburbs, the will of Heaven has not yet been understood. If one reveres principle, the world will be at peace. The scholar is the one who holds the riverbanks, but the master is the one who has not yet considered virtue. The teacher has defined the techniques. The teacher, however, is one who has not yet pondered virtue. (289)

This passage is a complex web of interrelated ideas, and the translation above attempts to capture the essence of the original while maintaining a degree of readability. The text is a series of connected aphorisms, and the central theme is the importance of principle (理 li) in maintaining social order and harmony.

The passage then talks about the consequences of not learning, and how a realm will retreat to the wilderness, a metaphor for regression. This chapter is a collection of aphorisms, and as such, it is not as easy to summarize as the previous chapters. The central theme, however, is the importance of ritual (li 禮) and propriety in governing a society, and the interconnectedness of all aspects of life.

The last part of the chapter, while still on the theme of harmony and balance, shifts the focus to the political realm.
